Title: Activation Tagging Identifies a Conserved MYB Regulator of Phenylpropanoid Biosynthesis

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Activation tag 35S enhancer (dominant activation); 35S promoter/35Se-based overexpression constructs
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: anthocyanins; flavonols; hydroxycinnamic acid esters; lignin
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: roots; stems; leaves; branches; cauline leaves; sepals; anthers; carpels; flowers; cell wall fraction
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Massively promotes phenylpropanoid accumulation, causing strong purple pigmentation and increased anthocyanins, flavonols, wall-bound hydroxycinnamic acids, and lignin-related monomers
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: phenylpropanoid biosynthesis, including flavonoid/anthocyanin branch and upstream central pathway activation
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: PAL; CHS; DFR; glutathione S-transferase gene
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates/strongly upregulates PAL, CHS, DFR, and GST transcript accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Activation tagging by T-DNA 35S enhancer; high light; drought; pathogen infection enhance visible pigmentation phenotype
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Requires TTG1 WD40 protein function for anthocyanin accumulation; likely acts in MYB regulatory complex
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Overexpressed in pap1-D mutant; otherwise not clearly defined in this paper
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Purple pigmentation and pathway activation observed throughout development in many vegetative and floral organs; transgene overexpression in Arabidopsis and tobacco causes widespread pigmentation
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: activation tagging; cosegregation with T-DNA/Basta resistance; plasmid rescue and cloning; RNA gel blot; genomic overexpression transformation in Arabidopsis; 35S:cDNA overexpression in tobacco; HPLC metabolite profiling; lignin analysis; PAL enzyme assay; genetic interaction with ttg1-1
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major positive regulator sufficient to hyperactivate broad phenylpropanoid biosynthesis and induce anthocyanin accumulation across tissues
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for metabolic engineering to boost phenylpropanoid products and pigmentation across species; candidate regulatory gene for engineering enriched phytochemical tissues and identifying orthologs in target crops/medicinal plants
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No other obvious morphological phenotypes reported under study conditions; anthocyanin production still requires TTG1 background
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PAP1 is identical to ATMYB75 except for a sequencing error in the reported ATMYB75 sequence; pap1-D results from enhancer insertion 5.1 kb downstream of PAP1; antisense PAP1 lines showed no visible phenotype, suggesting redundancy
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Activation tagging caused massive overexpression of PAP1 and dominant purple pigmentation in Arabidopsis. 2. PAP1 overexpression strongly increased expression of PAL, CHS, DFR, and GST, indicating broad activation of phenylpropanoid biosynthesis. 3. PAP1 increased accumulation of anthocyanins, flavonols, wall-bound hydroxycinnamic acids, PAL activity, and lignin monomers. 4. PAP1 function was sufficient to induce pigmentation in transgenic Arabidopsis and tobacco, but anthocyanin accumulation required TTG1.

Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes anthocyanin pigmentation in Arabidopsis and tobacco when overexpressed, with somewhat weaker effect than PAP1
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Likely functions in a conserved MYB regulatory system similar to PAP1, but direct complex evidence not shown in this paper
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Overexpression produces purple leaves and stems in Arabidopsis and enhanced pigmentation in tobacco vegetative tissues and flowers
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: sequence homology analysis; 35S:cDNA overexpression in Arabidopsis and tobacco; visible pigmentation phenotyping
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Positive MYB regulator sufficient to trigger anthocyanin accumulation across species upon overexpression
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential engineering gene for inducing anthocyanin pigmentation and phenylpropanoid enrichment in heterologous species
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Pigmentation effect appears somewhat weaker than PAP1; no additional morphological penalties reported
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PAP2 corresponds to ATMYB90 and is highly homologous to PAP1; similar overexpression phenotype suggests functional redundancy with PAP1
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PAP2 is a close homolog of PAP1/ATMYB90. 2. 35S-driven PAP2 overexpression caused purple pigmentation in Arabidopsis leaves and stems. 3. PAP2 also enhanced pigmentation in tobacco, supporting conserved regulatory activity. 4. Similarity of phenotype to PAP1 suggests partially redundant MYB regulators of anthocyanin biosynthesis.
